Brazil has a new funk star named Anitta. She has won millions of fans by bringing favela music into the mainstream. But there is now a debate about her skin colour.

People who fight discrimination and other writers say the music industry’s fastest rising star had to give up looking black to reach a mostly white, middle-class market.

The debate grew after “before and after” photos were shared. They show Anitta’s skin looking much lighter since she signed a deal with Warner.

In the first photo, before she was famous, she looks darker. In the second photo, used for marketing after she became famous, she looks paler. Some people wonder if this came from whitening products, cosmetic surgery, or more likely, Photoshop. The big difference made people talk again about whether you need to be pale to succeed in Brazil.

Jarid Arraes, a psychology student and blogger, wrote a post. She said the change in Anitta’s image shows hidden discrimination in media and marketing. She said many people do not accept that they are racist, and they think Brazil is a multiracial democracy, but the numbers show it is not. She said whitening shows a society that does not support diversity. She said people often connect “white” with rich, nice, successful, and good, and see “black” as the opposite.

Anitta was born Larissa de Macedo Machado. As a child, she sang in a church choir. As a teenager, she became known in Rio de Janeiro’s baile funk scene as a dancer and singer.

She later became very famous with an album and a big hit song, Show das Poderosas. The song reached number one and got 52 million views on YouTube.

Many fans love her as a pop idol with catchy songs and a strong message. The people who support her present her as a bridge between the mostly black and mixed-race poor communities on Rio’s hills and the richer and whiter areas below.

She has made baile funk less extreme for a wider audience. She reduced sexual dancing, “gangsta” talk, and explicit lyrics. Now people ask if she, or her marketing team, changed her image too much to attract a richer group of buyers. Arraes said this can pressure pop stars to change their looks, like straightening curly hair or getting a nose job, and that it can hurt self-esteem.

This topic is sensitive in Brazil, which has many mixed-race people. Brazil was one of the last big countries to ban slavery. It has the largest population of African descent outside Africa. But in Brazil, colour often matters more than race and family background. Even though Brazil aims to be a multiracial democracy, skin tone is clearly linked to inequality.

In Brazilian cities, white workers earn about twice as much as workers of African descent. Until 2011, black or mixed-race students also spent about two years less in school on average.

The government says the gap is getting smaller because of quota systems for university places and other affirmative action. But the difference is still easy to see. Most business and government leaders are white, while most low-paid jobs are done by black and mixed-race workers. In rich areas like Ipanema and Gávea, you are much more likely to see black nannies pushing strollers with white toddlers than the other way around.

Colour is hard to define. In the 2010 census, people who called themselves white were a minority for the first time. Out of 197 million people, 82 million said they were “pardu” (mixed race), 15 million said black, two million said Asian, and 0.5% said indigenous.

Sylvio Ferreira, a psychology teacher at the Federal University of Pernambuco, said Anitta won middle-class fans by taking a rebellious style and making it calmer and easier for everyone to accept. He said this was not done by making her “whiter.” He said it was a change in where she made her art: from the poorer edges of society to the centre.

Other people say the colour issue is too big. Maycon de Mattos Batista, a financial analyst who worked with Anitta when she was an intern, said her image changed a lot, but not her colour. He said it was more about makeup, hair styling, and clothing. He said he did not think she was pressured. He said she always liked to show off, sing, and dance, and that this natural style helped her succeed.

Leandro Silva de Souza, a racial equality activist in Salvador, said the prejudice was not mainly from the public, but from music producers and media leaders. He said the public showed they cared about music itself when they chose Ellen Oléria, a black lesbian, as the recent winner of the talent show The Voice Brazil.

The Guardian could not reach Anitta for comment. But in a recent interview, she said identity should be chosen by the person. She said a powerful woman does not need to be beautiful, but can be marvellous because of her attitude. She said her work tries to show that we can be who we want.

Author Philip Pullman says illegal downloading is theft. He says it is like reaching into someone’s pocket and stealing a wallet. In an article for Index on Censorship, Pullman defends copyright laws. He criticizes internet users who download music or books without paying.

Pullman writes that new technology can seem so impressive that people do not notice the wrong thing they are doing. He says it is wrong for anyone to steal an artist’s work and not be punished.

His article was written after music industry leaders met British Prime Minister David Cameron at Downing Street. They talked about web piracy.

Pullman, who wrote the His Dark Materials trilogy, says authors and musicians often work for years with little money and little attention. He says that when their work finally becomes popular, their chance to earn a living can be taken away by illegal downloading. He says the rule is simple: if we enjoy someone’s work, we should pay for it.

Pullman is writing in the next issue of the campaign group’s magazine in a dialogue with Cathy Casserly. She is the chief executive of Creative Commons. Creative Commons offers open content licences that let creators control copyright in a different way. Casserly says copyright has many problems because it was made “in an analogue age”. She says copyright can stop people from sharing, building on, and remixing each other’s work.

Casserly says artists need to think of new ways to distribute and monetize their work. She quotes science fiction writer Cory Doctorow, who said: “My problem is not piracy, it’s obscurity.”

Index on Censorship agrees with her. The magazine’s editor, Rachael Jolley, says current copyright laws do not work well in the digital age and might make consumers into criminals. She says new ways are needed so artists, writers, and musicians can earn a living.

The debate is very active, and illegal downloading is very large. Data from Ofcom, the UK communications regulator, suggests that in the UK, between November 2012 and January 2013, people illegally copied 280 million music tracks. They also copied 52 million TV shows, 29 million films, 18 million ebooks, and 7 million software or games files.

Ofcom says 18% of internet users over age 12 say they recently pirated content. Also, 9% say they fear being caught. Pullman writes that it is still surprising how easily and quickly people can get music as MP3 downloads, even to people who have been building up their iTunes list for some time.

After the Downing Street meeting, Cameron appointed Conservative MP Mike Weatherley as his adviser on this issue. A spokesman for the BPI, the record industry trade body, said Weatherley strongly supports copyright and the artists and creative producers it protects. The spokesman said they hope Weatherley and the prime minister will support copyright in the work of the UK’s intellectual property office.

Author Philip Pullman says illegal downloading is theft. He says it is like putting your hand in someone’s pocket and stealing their wallet. He wrote this in an article for Index on Censorship. Pullman is president of the Society of Authors. He strongly supports copyright laws. He is very critical of people who download music or books without paying.

Pullman writes that the technology looks very impressive, so some people do not see that what they are doing is wrong. He says it is shocking that someone can steal an artist’s work and not be punished. He repeats that it is theft, just like stealing a wallet.

His article came after music industry leaders met British Prime Minister David Cameron at Downing Street. They talked about web piracy.

Pullman, who wrote the His Dark Materials trilogy, says authors and musicians often work for years in poverty and without being known. They work hard to make something that audiences enjoy. But when they finally succeed, he says they can lose the chance to earn a living from it because people take it for free. He ends by saying the rule is simple: if we want to enjoy someone’s work, we should pay for it.

In the next issue of the group’s magazine, Pullman is in a discussion with Cathy Casserly. She is the chief executive of Creative Commons. Creative Commons offers open content licences that let creators control how they use copyright. Casserly says many things are wrong with copyright, which was made in an “analogue age.” She says copyright often stops people from sharing, building on, and remixing each other’s work in many ways.

She says artists need to think of new ways to share their work and make money from it. She quotes science fiction writer Cory Doctorow, who said: “My problem is not piracy, it’s obscurity.”

Index on Censorship agrees. The magazine’s editor, Rachael Jolley, said today’s copyright laws do not work well in the digital age and could make ordinary consumers into criminals. She said we need new ways for artists, writers, and musicians to earn money from their work.

Many people download illegally. Data from Ofcom (the independent regulator and competition authority for the UK communications industries) suggests that in the UK, between November 2012 and January 2013, 280 million music tracks were pirated. Also, 52 million TV shows, 29 million films, 18 million ebooks, and 7 million software or games files were pirated.

Ofcom has said 18% of internet users aged over 12 admit they have recently pirated content. Another 9% say they are afraid of getting caught. Pullman writes that it is still amazing how quickly people can get music as MP3 downloads, especially for people who have been building up their iTunes list for some time.

After the Downing Street meeting, Cameron appointed Conservative MP Mike Weatherley as his adviser on this issue. A spokesman for the BPI, the record industry trade body, said Mike Weatherley strongly supports copyright and the artists and creative producers that it protects. The spokesman said they hope Weatherley’s influence, and the prime minister’s support, will affect the approach of the UK’s intellectual property office.

This invention could be as useful as Trevor Baylis’s wind-up radio in the 1990s, before the internet. It is a cheap light that uses gravity to make free power. It could help stop the use of dangerous kerosene lamps in Africa and India.

British designer Patrick Hunt tried the usual way to get money from banks or venture capital firms. But he had trouble. He said getting funding was slow, complicated, unproven, and people did not want to take a risk.

So he tried crowdfunding on a US website called Indiegogo, which had just started in the UK. In five days, he reached his goal and raised £36,200. By the end of 2012, his campaign was so popular that in 40 days he raised £400,000.

The LED light uses a dynamo. The dynamo is powered when a 10kg bag of rocks slowly falls. The weight is attached to the light and lifted to about 2m. As it slowly drops to the ground, it makes enough power for 30 minutes of light.

Hunt is getting ready to make the light in China. Before making many more, he will send 1,000 lights to Africa to test the market. He hopes to later produce millions.

He is part of a new group of entrepreneurs who use crowdfunding to get money. Another new site is InvestingZone, which connects rich people with start-up entrepreneurs.

Indiegogo does not give shares. Instead, people who donate can get “perks” at different donation levels. People who helped fund Hunt’s light could feel good about helping poor people, and they also got their own light.

Danae Ringelmann, a co-founder of Indiegogo, says the “gravity light” shows how crowdfunding can work in a fair way and can test an idea. She says money can be fast and efficient because it is not about knowing people in a bank or a venture capital firm. It is about proving your value to customers and fans, getting them to support your idea, and paying for it.

She also says that even if an idea does not get funding, it is still useful to test it. Then you can learn faster and not waste too much time.

Ringelmann lives in the US and first worked as a Wall Street analyst. In 2008, she quit and used her skills to help friends in the arts raise money.

At first, the website focused on films. It launched at the Sundance Film Festival that year.

Five years later, it was raising about $2m each week for new businesses that were starting or growing. In December 2012, it added services in euros and pounds to expand in Europe. It says Britain is its third biggest market. International activity has risen 41% since December.

There are many competitors, such as Kickstarter, Seedrs, and Funding Circle. But Ringelmann says Indiegogo is different because anyone can start a campaign. No project is seen as too strange.

Indiegogo takes a 4% fee from successful campaigns. If a campaign does not reach its goal, users can choose to return all the money to donors for free, or keep the money but pay a 9% fee.

A British woman, Lauren Pears, raised £100,000 on the site to open a “cat café” in London. It is called Lady Dinah’s Cat Emporium. It has not opened yet, but it is described as a place where people can sit in a comfortable wingback chair with hot tea, a book, and a cat.

Ringelmann says some venture capitalists reject ideas because they do not know if people really want them. They may think it could be a gadget that nobody cares about.

She says entrepreneurs can run an Indiegogo campaign, even without launching the product, and the campaign can show proof that people want it. Then venture capitalists may believe there is a real market.

She says crowdfunding lets you test who your customers are, what price works, and which features people want. It can also help you find new ways to make money and get important feedback.

Because of her Wall Street experience and helping 100,000 businesses and services raise money, Ringelmann gives advice to new entrepreneurs.

She says ideas are common. What matters is doing the work well. She says if you are afraid someone will steal your idea and do it better and faster, then you may not be the right person to do it. She says you need confidence, speed, and a willingness to learn.

Ringelmann says Indiegogo’s move into Europe, and a deal with a web payment provider, are part of a dream to make finance open to more people. Payments will work through local card services like Maestro in the UK and Carte Bleue in France, and also through PayPal. More than 7,000 fundraising campaigns are live on the site.

Crowdfunding has grown as an alternative to banks, but it does not interest all big investors. Many big investors want a stake in a business. That may start to change in the UK with the launch of InvestingZone.

The LED light uses a dynamo. The dynamo is powered when a 10kg bag of rocks slowly drops down. The bag is attached to the light and lifted to about 2m high. As it falls to the ground, it makes enough power for half an hour of light.

Hunt is getting ready to make the product in China. He will test the market again by sending 1,000 lights to Africa before full mass production. He hopes mass production will be millions of units.

He is part of a new wave of entrepreneurs who use the fast-growing crowdfunding industry for money. Another new site is InvestingZone, which connects wealthy people with start-up entrepreneurs.

Indiegogo does not give shares. Instead, it lets users offer “perks” for different donation levels. People who helped fund Hunt’s light could feel good about helping poorer people, and they also got their own light.

Danae Ringelmann, a co-founder of Indiegogo, says the “gravity light” shows how crowdfunding can be fair and can test an idea. She says it is fast and efficient because it is not about knowing the right people at a bank or a venture capital firm. It is about proving the idea to customers and fans, and getting them to support it with money.

She also says even ideas that do not get funding are worth testing. If it is not a good idea, you find out sooner and learn faster.

Ringelmann lives in the US. She first worked as a Wall Street analyst. In 2008 she quit and used her skills to help friends in the arts raise money.

At first, the site focused on films. It launched at the Sundance Film Festival that year.

Five years later, the site was raising about $2m a week for new businesses that were starting or growing. In December 2012 it launched euro and sterling services to grow on this side of the Atlantic. It says Britain is its third biggest market. International activity is up 41% since December.

There are many competitors, like Kickstarter, Seedrs, and Funding Circle. But Ringelmann says Indiegogo is different because anyone can start a campaign. No project is seen as too strange.

The site charges a 4% fee for successful campaigns. If a campaign does not reach its target, users can choose to refund all the money to contributors at no cost, or keep all the money raised and pay a 9% fee.

A British woman, Lauren Pears, raised £100,000 on the site to open a “cat café” in London. It is called Lady Dinah’s Cat Emporium. It has not opened yet, but it is described as a place where people can sit in a comfortable wingback chair, have a hot cup of tea, read a book, and be with a cat.

Ringelmann says some campaigns are rejected by venture capitalists because they say the idea might not be something people want, and it could be a gadget nobody cares about. She says entrepreneurs can run an Indiegogo campaign, even without launching the project, and the campaign can show proof that a market exists. She says it lets people test the market, pricing, and features, find new revenue streams, and get important feedback.

With her Wall Street experience, and after helping 100,000 businesses and services raise money, Ringelmann has advice for new entrepreneurs. She says ideas are common, and success depends on doing the work well. She says if you are scared someone will steal your idea and do it better and faster, then you are not the right person to carry it out. She says you need confidence to move fast and learn.

Ringelmann says expansion into Europe, and a deal with a web transaction provider, are part of a dream to make finance more open to everyone. Crowdfunding as an option besides banks has grown, but it is less attractive to big investors. They usually want a stake in a promising business. This could start to change in the UK with the launch of InvestingZone.

Kenton Cool usually likes to talk, but now he can hardly speak. Climbing high mountains has made his voice rough. He is in Kathmandu, the capital of Nepal. He flew down from Everest base camp that morning.

Cool is thinking about a surprising set of climbs he finished the weekend before. Early on Saturday, he reached the top of Nuptse. Nuptse is the first and lowest of three main summits in the Everest “horseshoe.” This horseshoe is around an icy valley called the Western Cwm.

That same day, he climbed to the top of Everest. He reached the summit in complete darkness early on Sunday. Then he and his climbing partner kept going. They climbed to the top of Lhotse on Monday morning. Lhotse is the third peak in this three-mountain challenge.

“It was a snatched opportunity,” he says. “For the first time since the late 1990s, there were fixed ropes on all three mountains. That doesn’t take away the physical achievement of what I did. I’ve set the bar at a certain level. But whoever comes along next will move the bar further and do it without ropes or bottled oxygen.”

Sixty years after people first climbed Everest, many stories look back to Edmund Hillary and Tenzing Norgay. They talk about an earlier time, before today’s commercialism and environmental damage. I asked Cool to look ahead and imagine what top climbers might do 60 years from now.

“I hate to think,” he says. He mentions the Swiss climber Ueli Steck. Steck left the mountain in April after what Cool calls “an altercation” with a crowd of Sherpas at Camp 2. Cool says Steck planned to climb Everest’s west ridge, first climbed in 1963. Then he would go down to the South Col and immediately climb Lhotse by a new route. He planned to do this without fixed ropes. “Ueli had been training like a machine,” Cool says. “He’s a climber in a class all his own. He’s technically brilliant but he had also taken his physical condition to an astronomic level. It would have been amazing to see what he could have done.”

What tourism will be like is a different question. One clue is a helicopter rescue done by Simone Moro, Steck’s climbing partner. Moro’s angry words helped start the confrontation at Camp 2. On Tuesday, Moro flew back to Everest in a high-powered helicopter. He rescued a sick climber at 7,800 metres.

This was the highest rescue ever done on Everest. It shows how quickly helicopter flights have increased in recent years. By 2073, the mountain could even have a helipad on the South Col. Tourists could be brought there while breathing bottled oxygen. For now, helicopters are changing what rescues are possible for both climbers and the many trekkers who go as far as base camp.

Mountain geographer and environmentalist Alton Byers says it is not clear if the Everest region can keep handling fast-growing tourism. He says climate change and tourism together are putting new pressure on the Sherpa homeland. Glaciers are shrinking, and some are disappearing. This is already having a big effect. “Everywhere you go, people are talking about how there’s less water. There’s less water for agriculture and less water for all the new lodges that are getting built.”

In the Sherpa town of Namche Bazaar, Byers says, a new five-mile pipeline is being built to bring water for tourists who want showers and flush toilets. The local stream has been polluted by human waste. It also does not have enough water when it is high season and the town is very crowded. “Every village is digging a pit just beyond the houses for garbage. Khumbu has the highest landfill sites in the world,” he says. Human waste at base camp is now handled well and taken away in plastic barrels. But Byers says the barrels are emptied into a huge pit a few hours down the valley. That pit could leak into the region’s water.

“These problems can be solved, but we need to get serious about it,” he says. “One climber can spend $85,000 climbing Everest. And that’s fine. But at some point we’re going to have to address these other priorities. For half a million dollars a year, you could solve most of them.”

Climate change is harder to deal with. Byers works with local conservation committees to plan for climate change. They often look for new water sources or use rainwater harvesting. Another danger is that glacial lakes are growing fast. These lakes could burst and flood the Sherpa homeland. “There’s going to come a time when people are going to have to get out of their way.”

Changing weather is also affecting tourism. More clouds during times that are usually clear are closing Lukla Airport more often. Lukla Airport is the main entry to the Everest region. A new road for 4x4s is being built to Lukla to keep tourists and money coming. But Byers worries the road building in Nepal is being done cheaply. He says this can cause serious soil erosion and landslides.

“Everest is the icon everyone knows,” he says. “It’s the canary in the coalmine that everyone understands. It’s the perfect laboratory for figuring out how to address some of these problems, like the impacts of climate change and tourism.”

That same day, he climbed to the top of Everest. He reached the summit in complete darkness early on Sunday. Then he and his climbing partner kept going. On Monday morning, they reached the summit of Lhotse, the third peak in this three-mountain challenge.

“It was a chance I grabbed,” he says. “For the first time since the late 1990s, there were fixed ropes on all three mountains. That does not remove the physical achievement of what I did. I set a high standard. But the next person may set it even higher, and do it without ropes or bottled oxygen.”

Sixty years after Everest was first climbed, many stories look back to Edmund Hillary and Tenzing Norgay. They remember a time before today’s commercial climbing and environmental damage. I asked Cool to think ahead and imagine what top climbers might do 60 years from now.

“I hate to think,” he says. He talks about the Swiss climber Ueli Steck. Steck left the mountain in April after what Cool calls “an altercation” with a crowd of Sherpas at Camp 2. Cool says Steck planned to climb Everest’s west ridge, first climbed in 1963. Then Steck planned to go down to the South Col and immediately climb Lhotse by a new route, without fixed ropes. “Ueli trained like a machine,” Cool says. “He is in a class of his own. He was very skilled, and his fitness was extremely high. It would have been amazing to see what he could have done.”

Tourism is another question. One sign is a helicopter rescue by Simone Moro, Steck’s climbing partner. Moro flew back to Everest on Tuesday in a powerful helicopter. He rescued a very sick climber at 7,800 metres.

It was the highest rescue ever done on Everest. It also shows how fast helicopter flights have increased in recent years. By 2073, there could even be a helipad on the South Col, bringing tourists who breathe bottled oxygen. For now, helicopters are changing how people can rescue both climbers and the many more trekkers who go as far as base camp.

Mountain geographer and environmentalist Alton Byers says it is not clear if the Everest region can keep handling fast-growing tourism. He says climate change and tourism together are creating new problems for the Sherpa homeland. Glaciers are shrinking, and some are disappearing. This is already having a big effect. “Everywhere you go, people talk about having less water,” he says. “There is less water for farming and less water for all the new lodges being built.”

Byers says that in the Sherpa town of Namche Bazaar, workers are building a new five-mile pipeline to bring water for the growing tourist need for showers and flush toilets. The local stream is polluted with human waste and does not give enough water. In high season, the town is extremely crowded. “Every village is digging a pit just beyond the houses for garbage. Khumbu has the highest landfill sites in the world,” he says. Human waste at base camp is now handled well and removed in plastic barrels. But Byers says the barrels are emptied into a huge pit a few hours down the valley, and it could leak into the region’s water.

“These problems can be solved, but we need to take them seriously,” he says. “One climber can spend $85,000 climbing Everest. That is fine. But at some point we must deal with these other needs. For half a million dollars a year, you could solve most of them.”

Climate change is harder. Byers works with local conservation committees to find and plan for the effects of climate change. They often look for new water sources or collect rainwater. Another danger is that glacial lakes are growing quickly. They can burst and flood the Sherpa homeland. “A time will come when people will have to get out of their way,” he says.

Changing weather is also affecting tourism. More clouds during times that are usually clear are closing Lukla Airport more often. Lukla Airport is the main entrance to the Everest region. A new road for 4x4s is being built to Lukla to keep tourists and money coming. But Byers worries the fast spread of roads in Nepal is being done cheaply, which can cause serious soil erosion and landslides.

“Everest is the famous mountain everyone knows,” he says. “It is the warning sign everyone understands. It is a perfect place to learn how to deal with problems like climate change and tourism.”

When the lottery balls fall and all six numbers match, it can feel like it is time to buy an Audi, book a holiday in the US, and call an estate agent. An analysis of lottery jackpot winners says many millionaires do things like this.

Since it started in 1994, the lottery has made 3,000 millionaires. Together they have won more than £8.5bn in total, with an average of £2.8m each. The study authors, Oxford Economics, say this money also helped create 3,780 more millionaires among the winners’ children, family, and friends.

Most winners (59%) stop working right away. But 19% keep doing their normal job, and 31% do unpaid voluntary work. The study says 98% of winners’ spending stayed in the UK. By spending on homes, vehicles, and holidays, it is estimated that each winner supports six full-time jobs for one year.

Winners added almost £750m to GDP and created more than £500m in tax for the Exchequer. Most of the money went on property. Winners spent £2.72bn on their main homes, and £170m paying off debts and mortgages.

Many winners wanted to keep an income, so £2.125bn was spent on investments. Gifts to family and friends were £1.17bn. Cars and holidays cost £680m.

Camelot, the company that runs the UK National Lottery, paid for the study to mark 3,000 winners. It used research from 100 winners who had won £1m or more. The study found that the 3,000 winners bought 7,958 houses or flats in the UK, about 2.7 each, spending £3.3bn. Most winners (82%) moved to a new main home, spending about £900,000 on average.

Many new homes had extra features. Almost a third (29%) bought a hot tub. A walk-in wardrobe was important for 28%. Almost a quarter (24%) chose a home behind electric gates. About 22% had a games room, and 7% put in a snooker table.

Bigger homes also needed help. About 30% hired a cleaner and 24% hired a gardener. A small number (5%) hired a beautician.

Audis were the most popular car for 16% of winners. Range Rovers and BMWs were also popular (11% each), as well as Mercedes (10%) and Land Rovers (5%). Winners spent £463m on 17,190 cars. The average price of the favourite car was £46,116.

Holidays were important too. Most winners (68%) chose five-star hotels overseas. The US was the top destination for 27%, followed by the Caribbean (9%). In the UK, caravan sales also gained. Over the past 18 years, 10% of millionaires bought a caravan, creating about £7.4m in sales.

Some winners (15%) started their own business. About 9% helped other people start businesses, and 6% invested in or bought other people’s businesses. The study says these businesses employ 3,195 people.

Andy Logan, who wrote the report, said a win spreads further than they expected. He said it changes the lives of friends and family, and each win also affects the UK economy because so much money is spent in the UK. He said each win can have a ripple effect for this generation and often the next.

Angela Erdmann never met her grandfather. He died in 1946, six years before she was born.

On Tuesday 8th April, 2014, she told the story of an amazing event. She got a message in a bottle 101 years after her grandfather threw it into the Baltic Sea. People think it may be the world’s oldest message in a bottle. A museum in Germany gave it to her, and the museum is now showing it.

Erdmann, 62, said it was very surprising. A man came to her door and said he had post from her grandfather. He said a message in a bottle had been found, and the name on the card was her grandfather’s. The visitor was a genealogical researcher. He found her in Berlin after the letter was given to the International Maritime Museum in Hamburg.

The brown beer bottle had been in the water for 101 years. A fisherman named Konrad Fischer found it while fishing in the Baltic Sea near the northern city of Kiel. Holger von Neuhoff, a curator at the museum, said it was the oldest bottled message he had seen. He said the museum has older documents without a bottle, but this one has both the bottle and the document. He said it was in very good condition.

Researchers believe Erdmann’s grandfather, Richard Platz, threw the bottle into the sea in 1913. He was hiking with a nature appreciation group. He was 20 years old.

People could not read much of the postcard. But the Berlin address on the front was clear. The card also asked the finder to send the note to his home address.

Erdmann said her grandfather put two stamps in the bottle, so the finder would not have to pay. But he did not think it would take 101 years.

Erdmann said she felt very moved. She did not know her grandfather because he died at age 54, six years before she was born.

She said she knew very little about him. She learned that he was a writer. She said he was open-minded, believed in freedom, and believed people should respect each other. She said he did a lot for young people. Later he travelled with his wife and two daughters. She said it was wonderful to learn where her roots came from.

Erdmann said she also likes culture and travelling around the world. She said she is open-minded too. She said what her grandfather taught his two daughters, her mother taught her, and she has taught it to her sons.

Even though she was happy to get the message, she said people should not throw bottles with messages into the sea today. She said the sea already has many bottles and much rubbish.

The message and bottle will be shown at Hamburg’s Maritime Museum until the beginning of May 2014. After that, experts will try to read the rest of the text. It is not clear what will happen to the bottle after that, but Erdmann hopes it will stay at the museum.

She said they want to add some photos with the bottle. This way, visitors can see the young man who threw the bottle into the water.

A thick layer of bird droppings covers a gold-painted railing at one of Britain’s most expensive homes. Pigeon skeletons lie among broken mirrors, and water runs through broken parts of the building. This house is called The Towers. It is a £30m palace on “Billionaires’ Row” in north London. Its damage has been kept secret until now.

The Towers is one of ten mansions in the middle of The Bishops Avenue. This street is part of London’s fast-rising housing market. These houses have been almost empty since they were bought about 25 years ago. It is believed they were bought for members of the Saudi Arabian royal family. Their Greek-style columns are breaking. Their swimming pools with mosaic tiles are full of broken pieces. Nature has taken over so much that owls have moved in.

This empty and broken look is seen along the street. Lloyds Bank says this is the second most expensive street in Britain. At the same time, many people in London struggle to buy a home because house prices rise 11.2% a year. But 16 mansions on the most expensive part of The Bishops Avenue are empty. Many are behind locked gates. Their windows are covered with steel bars, and their gardens are overgrown. Guard dogs patrol the grounds.

Across the street, there is another empty mansion worth £18m, with broken windows and special paint to stop people climbing. Another house has metal bars on the windows and has been sold for £20m.

Still, prices keep rising. Dryades, a mansion that was owned until recently by a Pakistani politician, sold for £12m in 2007. It is believed to be worth about £30m today. Heath Lodge, where fashion tycoon Aristos Constantinou was murdered in 1984 by a silver bullet, is worth £13m today. It was sold in the late 1970s for £400,000.

For people who struggle to pay for housing in one of the world’s most expensive cities, these empty homes can feel painful to see. A security guard said it was very upsetting to watch so much space—enough to house dozens of people—falling apart.

Rich royals from Nigeria and Saudi Arabia were among the first to come to this curved road near Hampstead Heath. Iranians came here after the fall of the shah. Now, Chinese buyers are following Russians and Kazakhs. They have paid millions to live on a street that estate agents say is as famous as the Champs Elysées and Rodeo Drive. Recently, two mansions were for sale for £65m and £38m. They promised Italian marble, lifts with leather walls, and luxury panic rooms.

But around these empty houses, stone fountains are breaking and lawns have turned into muddy wetlands. Inside one house, water drips through a huge crystal chandelier onto a thick carpet that is rotting under parts of a collapsed ceiling. Moss grows through broken bricks, and mirrored tiles are scattered in a bathroom. The swimming pool has about a foot of dirty, salty water, and flowers grow through the tiles. Wooden slats in the sauna bend outward.

The most dramatic ruin is The Towers. It is a large mansion among many acres of hornbeams, oaks, and limes. Pigeons fill its big halls. Water pours through three floors, turning the walls bright green with algae, and drops into a huge empty basement swimming pool. Unopened wooden crates marked “bullet-proof glass” show that the old owners were worried about safety.

Today, very few people live on The Bishops Avenue all the time. A security guard outside one mansion said the owners were not there. Another guard outside Royal Mansion would not say if anyone was home. A staff member at another mansion warned the Guardian about the guard dogs. Magdy Adib Ishak-Hannah, an Egyptian-born private healthcare mogul worth £45m, said he is one of the few people who live there permanently.

He said, “It’s not a friendly place where you can talk over the fence. I have never seen what my neighbours look like. Next door, a Saudi princess spent £35m on a new house and I’ve never seen her. About three houses are lived in all the time, and half the properties are used three to six months a year. The other half, who knows if they come or not?”

These broken, very expensive homes show a housing culture where the world’s richest people treat British property as investments. One Hyde Park, an apartment building in Knightsbridge, is another example. More than half the flats there are registered with the council as empty or second homes.

Even so, people on the avenue are talking about building £5m apartments instead of £50m mansions, to bring people back.

Anil Varma, a local property developer, wants to rebuild one of the most valuable sites on the avenue. He plans 20 apartments with a concierge, maid service, a 25-metre pool, a spa, and a cinema.

He said, “If you build a big house and try and sell for £30m to £40m, it won’t sell. Locals won’t buy, so you have to bring in overseas buyers.”

But using the empty homes to help with the housing crisis still seems far away. Andrew Harper, a local Conservative councillor, laughed when asked if some of the empty homes could become affordable housing. He said the land price would be too high.

He said, “Very wealthy people own property there. Sometimes they live in them and sometimes they don’t.”

Now, Chinese buyers are following Russians and Kazakhs who have paid millions for an address that estate agents say is as famous as the Champs Elysées and Rodeo Drive. Recently, two mansions were for sale for £65m and £38m, offering Italian marble, lifts with leather padding, and luxury panic rooms.

But on the grounds of the empty homes, stone fountains are breaking and lawns have turned into muddy bogs. Wooden boards bend away from the sauna.

The most dramatic ruin is The Towers. It is a grand home set among many trees, including hornbeams, oaks, and limes. Pigeons fill its tall halls, and algae has turned the walls bright green. Water pours down through three floors and drops into a huge, empty basement swimming pool. Unopened wooden boxes marked “bullet-proof glass” show that the earlier owners worried about security.

Another guard, outside Royal Mansion, would not say if anyone was home. A worker at another house only warned the Guardian about the guard dogs. It was a beautiful summer evening, and I decided to go for a swim near Doolin Pier in County Clare, Ireland, where I moved in 2012. A woman was already in the water with Dusty, a dolphin. Dusty often swims with a group of people, and she seems to know them well.

Dusty came to Doolin around 2008. Since then, hundreds of people have swum with her, so many people think she is completely tame. She was even in an Irish tourist-board advertisement, where a girl says she wants to touch a dolphin.

That evening, the woman was tickling Dusty’s tummy, and it looked safe and friendly. About 20 tourists and local people stood on the pier watching. Soon after I got into the water, Dusty left the woman and suddenly became wild. I later learned that she is very territorial when she is with someone. Her tail was slapping hard. At first I thought she was just showing off, but then I realized she might be angry.

I knew I needed to get out, so I swam toward the pier. But almost instantly, Dusty crashed into me with her snout. It was very strong and very painful, and she was moving fast. I was thrown forward.

Everyone on the pier stared at me in shock. Dusty stayed in the water next to me, still slapping her tail. That was the scariest part. I thought, if she hits me with her tail, I could go under the water and die.

I reached the pier, but I could not climb out because I was hurt. I felt pure terror. I shouted for help, and a man reached in and pulled me onto the steps. Then another man arrived and said he was an orthopaedic surgeon who specialized in marine trauma. He had been driving into Doolin when he saw what was happening.

I was very cold and very worried because I did not know how bad my injuries were. My biggest fear was internal bleeding. He checked me and told me he could not feel signs of internal bleeding, but he thought I probably had broken bones. Later I learned I had six spinal fractures, three broken ribs, and a damaged lung.

I stayed in hospital for five days. I wore a back brace for several weeks. I could not work for five months, and I had limited movement, stiffness, and pain. Then doctors said I had post-traumatic stress. After the near-death experience, I became anxious about everything and overreacted in ways I never had before. I felt like people were looking at me the wrong way. Loud noises were hard for me, and I had memory loss.

Three months before the accident, I had opened a health-food shop, but I had to give it up because I could not work anymore. It was the hardest year ever, but now it is behind me. I had craniosacral therapy, osteopathy, and massage, and now I am building up my own osteopathy practice. I understand my patients better because I have been a patient myself.

I am thankful that I am healthy, and I want to stop other people from getting hurt. People have a happy idea about dolphins and trust them. Who thinks a dolphin would attack a person? If a dangerous animal comes at you with its teeth showing, it is clearly scary. But dolphins have a wide smile, so they do not look threatening.

I am not angry with Dusty. I respect her. But I was in her territory, and she is a wild and unpredictable animal. People need to understand that. Many people come to swim with her and do not know how dangerous it can be. People said my injuries were among several that summer.

After the man pulled me out of the water, Dusty swam away. Then she came back and floated upright next to me, looking at me. We looked into each other’s eyes, and I felt she was truly sorry. She seemed like a different dolphin, and the anger was gone. The people on the pier were amazed. After that moment, I did not feel the terror anymore. I felt at peace with her.

From glow-in-the-dark trees to bike storage under the ground and bins that run on solar power, people are trying unusual ideas to make cities nicer places to live.

City life has many good points, but it is getting harder to live in a way that is good for the planet. City people deal with pollution, traffic, and fewer green spaces. Inventors are now offering some of the stranger ideas to improve city life.

Pop-up parks
Some cities seem built more for cars than for people. A pop-up park is a simple idea: use an empty parking space, a little money, and a few plants to make a tiny park. The PARK(ing) project started as an art experiment in San Francisco and then spread around the world. People have also tried temporary city farms and ecology demonstrations. The movement has a day of action every September.

Subterranean storage
Not everyone in cities uses a car. Bikes are becoming more popular, but they need to be stored safely. A Tokyo engineering firm called Giken made an idea called an “eco-cycle, anti-seismic underground bicycle park.” It is a round storage space that is seven metres wide and goes deep into the ground. It can hold 204 bikes. Bike owners press a button, and the system brings the bike back up in about 13 seconds.

Glow-in-the-dark trees
Some people think of Christmas decorations when they think of glowing trees, but Daan Roosegaarde thinks differently. This Dutch designer-artist has created an experimental “bioluminescent” plant. The technology combines DNA from glowing marine bacteria with part of a plant to make a glow like a jellyfish. Tests are happening to make a large-scale version. Roosegaarde hopes it could one day replace normal street lights.

Footfall harvesting
In West Ham in east London, many commuters and shoppers cross an elevated walkway near the underground station. Most people do not notice that the floor feels slightly springy. That small movement in the rubber surface helps power the streetlights above. The floor has smart tiles that capture energy from footsteps and turn it into electricity. The UK firm Pavegen made this idea and has also installed similar systems at London’s Heathrow Airport and other places.

Supertrees
Singapore’s Gardens by the Bay has man-made “supertrees.” They are steel-framed and can be up to 50 metres high. Flowers and ferns grow on them, and their canopies help take in and spread out heat. They can also collect rainwater and help provide air ventilation for two “climate-controlled biomes” (large conservatories) below. Eleven of the 18 trees also have solar panels on their “branches.”

James Hamblin is a senior editor at The Atlantic magazine. In his series “If Our Bodies Could Talk,” he tried a no-showering challenge. He wanted to see what happens when we clean our bodies too much. He took fewer showers. When he did shower, he did not use shampoo or soap.

He found something many people have found: when we scrub a lot with soap and body wash, our skin tries harder to get back to normal. Showering can wash away the skin’s natural oils and bacteria. Hot water and cleaning products can make this even stronger.

Have you ever felt very tight and dry after a shower? That can happen because the skin’s natural moisture gets washed away. Our skin also has millions of helpful bacteria, like our gut does. Frequent rubbing and scrubbing can destroy these bacteria. When the bacteria grow back, the new microbes may make more body odor. So, showering too often may sometimes make you smell more. If you stop showering and using soap, your skin may go through an early adjustment period. After that, the skin often becomes more balanced, makes less oil, and healthy bacteria can grow again.

After his experiment, Hamblin felt that the human body can work well on its own. It is not only about smell or looks. Dermatologists say that using less soap can help acne and eczema. Taking fewer showers and using fewer cleaning products can also help the environment. The average shower lasts seven minutes and uses 65 litres of water. That is 65 litres of clean, drinkable water mixed with soap and sent down the drain every day, sometimes more than once.

Clean water is becoming harder to ignore as California has another summer of drought. It is becoming clear that clean water is one of the world’s most valuable resources, and it may soon be in short supply. Also, all the body wash bottles can hurt the environment. These are strong reasons to let your body be more natural.

If this idea makes you feel nervous because you remember being close to people who smell, relax. Many people who cut back on cleaning still use deodorant if they need it. People’s natural smell is different, and it can change with diet, hydration, and exercise. Also, washing hands with soap is still recommended to help stop infectious diseases from spreading.

You do not need to stop all at once like Hamblin did. But between cleaning many times a day and never showering, there is a middle choice. Reduce. Skip a few showers, use less soap, and let your skin’s helpful bacteria grow.

Clean water is becoming more important, especially as California faces another summer of drought. It is becoming clear that clean water is very valuable and may soon be hard to get. Also, body wash bottles create waste. These are strong reasons to let your body be more natural.

If this idea makes you feel nervous because you remember being close to people who did not shower much, do not worry. Many people who shower less still use deodorant if they need it. Everyone’s natural smell is different, and it can change with diet, hydration, and exercise. Also, washing hands with soap is still recommended to help stop the spread of infectious diseases.

You do not have to stop showering suddenly like Hamblin did. But you can make a simple change: reduce. Skip some showers, use less soap, and let your skin’s natural bacteria grow.

text:
Dan Sullivan was late to breakfast with the Guardian. He was late because police stopped him while he was riding his motorcycle.

Sullivan says he works too much. He misses dinner dates. He forgets to buy presents.

So, like many people in Silicon Valley, the 27-year-old venture capitalist made a start-up to help. It is called BetterBoyfriend.me. For about $70 a month, it sends a present each month to girlfriends and wives.

Sullivan works in finance. He has been testing the service quietly. About 350 boyfriends have signed up. They bring in about $17,000 each month. Most members are his Harvard and investor friends. They include venture capitalists, founders, and workers at companies like Apple, Google, and Facebook. Their girlfriends have been getting presents from Sullivan for the last year.

Each month, members choose from seven possible gifts, like chocolate, tea sets, or manicures. The package is sent to the boyfriend with no brand name and no label.

Sullivan tests ideas by buying Facebook ads to see what happens. BetterBoyfriend.me became popular fast. Sullivan said it solved a problem many people have.

BetterBoyfriend.me was not his first idea. For a few days, he thought about a dog-sharing idea. He said he saw rich guys looking unhappy while walking dogs. He thought a dog is only valuable 5% of the time. He wondered if someone could watch dogs and other people could rent them.

Another start-up is called InvisibleBoyfriend. It lets users create a fake social partner who is always available to talk. The result is a “boyfriend” who can text with you all day.

The founder, Kyle Tabor, started it to help people stop unwanted attention. But he found that people started to feel close to their computer-made partners. He said many users want companionship through conversation, not just “proof” of a relationship.

For Sullivan and Tabor, a surprise was how real the customer relationships became. Sullivan says he now feels like a kind of relationship adviser for the boyfriends.

Sullivan says he made mistakes. At first, the packages had receipts with his name, Dan Sullivan. One boyfriend wrote to him and said, “Listen, dude, she’s not mad but Cynthia found out.”

About 50% of the women who get BetterBoyfriend.me packages know Sullivan is involved. Sullivan says it relates to age. He thinks that after people have been married for a while, they do not keep many secrets.

Over the year, Sullivan says he got to know the boyfriends well. They even delivered a package to a hospital delivery room. He said the important thing is to remember his relationship is with the boyfriend.

When he first started the company, he put BetterBoyfriend.me tags on flower bouquets. He went to Dolores Park, a popular place in the Mission District.

He looked for couples and gave the flowers to the girl. The boyfriend did not like that. Sullivan said it made sense. So he changed his plan and started giving the flowers to the boyfriends instead.

Opposition to Western Australia’s shark cull has grown. Thousands of people went to beaches across Australia to ask the state’s premier to stop the policy. RSPCA Australia and Richard Branson, who owns Virgin Atlantic, also spoke against it.

The policy catches and kills sharks longer than three metres. It started after what the state government called an “unprecedented” number of shark attacks on Western Australia’s coast. A 35-year-old surfer was killed in November 2013. He was the sixth person to die from a shark attack in two years.

But the Shark Attack File says Australia has averaged one shark-related death a year for the last 50 years.

Kate Faehrmann from the Sea Shepherd Conservation Society spoke at a protest in Perth. She said the policy will not work. She said drumlines, used to catch sharks, can kill many animals. They can catch sharks of any size, and also dolphins, turtles, and other animals. She said this is why the community does not want the policy.

Thousands protested at Perth’s Cottesloe Beach and Sydney’s Manly Beach. Hundreds protested at Glenelg in south-west Adelaide, and at beaches in Victoria and Queensland. Faehrmann said the protests showed many Australians want sharks protected. She said that even with fear from the movie Jaws, people are coming out to say the ocean is the sharks’ home and they do not want them killed.

Surfer Anthony Joyce, who once had his foot caught in a shark’s mouth, said the number of sharks killed would not make a difference.

The state government has refused to give an updated total of sharks killed. There have been reports of sharks smaller than three metres being released after being caught on drumlines. Drumlines are floating drums tied to the sea bed, with baited hooks under them.

Conservationists say there is no evidence the cull will reduce shark attacks on humans. They say no earlier cull used only drumlines. Researchers at the University of Western Australia say the recent increase in shark attacks may be linked to the state having the fastest-growing population in Australia, not to more sharks.

Richard Peirce, chairman of the UK charity the Shark Trust, said the cull would not work and might bring more predators near the coast. He said it is sad the government did not try other options instead of killing animals in a way that can harm many species. He also said that leaving drumlines in overnight could attract other predators to the area because sharks and other animals may be hooked and injured.

Worldwide in 2012, there were 80 unprovoked shark attacks, and seven were fatal. Humans kill nearly 100m sharks each year. RSPCA Australia said it believes the cull is not justified. It said there is no evidence that more attacks are caused by more sharks. It said the change fits with population and human behaviour, meaning more people are in the water.

Richard Branson told Fairfax Radio the policy was backfiring. He said Premier Colin Barnett may have thought it would help tourism, but he thinks it will do the opposite. He said it advertises a problem that is not big, and could stop people from coming to Perth and the countryside around it. He said it may only make people worry for no reason.

Opposition to Western Australia’s shark cull has grown. Thousands of people went to beaches across Australia to ask the state’s premier to stop the policy. RSPCA Australia and Richard Branson, who owns Virgin Atlantic, also spoke against it.

The policy involves catching and killing sharks longer than three metres. It started after what the state government called an “unprecedented” number of shark attacks on the Western Australia coast. A 35-year-old surfer was killed in November 2013. He was the sixth person to die from a shark attack in two years.

But the Shark Attack File says that Australia has averaged one shark-related death a year for the last 50 years.

Kate Faehrmann from the Sea Shepherd Conservation Society spoke at a protest in Perth. She said the policy will not work. She said drumlines, used to catch sharks, are not selective. They can catch and kill sharks of any size, and also dolphins, turtles, and other animals. She said this is why the community does not want the policy.

Protests happened at Perth’s Cottesloe Beach and Sydney’s Manly Beach. There were also protests at Glenelg in south-west Adelaide, and at beaches in Victoria and Queensland. Faehrmann said the protests showed many Australians want sharks protected. She said that even with fear from movies like Jaws, many people are saying the ocean belongs to sharks too and they do not want sharks killed.

Anthony Joyce, a surfer who once had his foot caught in a shark’s mouth, said killing these sharks will not make a difference.

The state government has not given a running count of how many sharks have been killed. There have been reports that sharks smaller than three metres were released after being caught. Drumlines are floating drums fixed to the sea floor, with bait on hooks below them.

Conservationists say there is no evidence the cull will reduce shark attacks on people. They say no earlier cull used only drumlines. Researchers at the University of Western Australia say the recent increase in attacks may be linked to Western Australia having the fastest-growing population in Australia, not to more sharks.

Richard Peirce from the UK charity the Shark Trust said the cull will not work and could bring more predators closer to the coast. He said leaving drumlines in the water overnight could attract other predators to the area because sharks and other animals may be hooked and hurt.

In 2012 worldwide, there were 80 unprovoked shark attacks, and seven were fatal. Humans kill nearly 100 million sharks each year. RSPCA Australia said it believes the cull is not justified. It said there is no evidence the rise in attacks is because there are more sharks. It said it fits better with changes in population and human behaviour, meaning more people are in the water.

Richard Branson told Fairfax Radio the policy could have the opposite effect on tourism. He said it may make people think there is a big problem when there is not, and it could stop people from visiting Perth and the surrounding countryside. He said it may only make people worry for no need.
